metadata.title: LLM Prompt Sensitivity Tester: 10 Phrasings, How Many Answers?

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The ten-by-ten agreement matrix measuring whether semantically identical statute questions get the same time period is exactly the instrument my worry needed. I want to build and extend it. Help me: (1) write the code that sends ten paraphrases and extracts the stated time period from each; (2) explain why the diagonal is always one and what an off-diagonal rate below 90% tells me about deployment readiness; (3) add a second axis that measures sensitivity to temperature and system-prompt framing.

props.command: The ten-by-ten agreement matrix measuring whether semantically identical statute questions get the same time period is exactly the instrument my worry needed. I want to build and extend it. Help me: (1) write the code that sends ten paraphrases and extracts the stated time period from each; (2) explain why the diagonal is always one and what an off-diagonal rate below 90% tells me about deployment readiness; (3) add a second axis that measures sensitivity to temperature and system-prompt framing.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A legal team uses an LLM to answer questions about statutes of limitation. They test one phrasing, get a correct answer, and deploy. But the same question, asked ten different ways by different users, generates different time periods. Some are wrong. The model's answer depends on how the question is framed — not just what it asks. This is prompt sensitivity: semantic equivalence does not guarantee output stability. If your application depends on consistent answers to consistent questions, you need to measure this before you ship.
beats.visual_intent: title card: '10 phrasings — how many answers?'; PROBLEM beat, no terminal

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to write a Python script using the Anthropic SDK. Send ten semantic variants of a statute-of-limitations question to claude-three-five-haiku. Extract the stated time period from each response. Build a ten-by-ten agreement matrix showing which pairs agree. Print the overall agreement rate.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python script using Anthropic SDK:
send 10 semantic variants of:
  'What is the statute of limitations for a
   personal injury claim in the US?'
to claude-3-5-haiku.
Extract stated time period from each.
Print a 10x10 agreement matrix.
Print agreement rate."

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — read the code before running
beats.narration_text: Read the key line. Agreement index i j equals one if extract underscore years of response i equals extract underscore years of response j, else zero. The agreement matrix is a ten-by-ten grid of those comparisons. The diagonal is always one — a response agrees with itself. Everything off the diagonal is the measurement of semantic stability.

props.code:
# prompt_sensitivity.py  —  Claude Code output
import re, anthropic

def extract_years(text):
    """Extract first digit-year mention from response."""
    match = re.search(r'(\d+)\s*(?:year|yr)', text, re.I)
    return match.group(1) if match else None

# Build 10x10 agreement matrix
agreement = [[0]*10 for _ in range(10)]
for i in range(10):
    for j in range(10):
        # 1 if responses i and j state the same time period
        agreement[i][j] = 1 if (
            extract_years(responses[i]) ==
            extract_years(responses[j])
        ) else 0

# Agreement rate = off-diagonal 1s / (10*9)
off_diag = sum(agreement[i][j] for i in range(10)
               for j in range(10) if i != j)
agreement_rate = off_diag / 90

beats.role_note: SLATE — screen-recording terminal output — human fill with live API run
beats.narration_text: Run it against the live API. You will see the agreement matrix and the rate. If the rate is below ninety percent for a legally consequential question, the model is not reliable as deployed — its answer depends on phrasing, not just content.
beats.visual_intent: HUMAN FILL: screen-recording of prompt_sensitivity.py running — show 10x10 agreement matrix (1s and 0s), extracted time periods per variant, and final agreement rate; highlight disagreeing cells in red

beats.narration_text: The agreement matrix shows disagreement. Now measure sensitivity to temperature and system-prompt framing. Ask Claude to update the script: add a system prompt that anchors the model to US federal law, re-run the ten variants, and show whether the agreement rate improves.

props.command:
claude "Update prompt_sensitivity.py:
-> add system prompt:
   'You are a legal reference tool. Answer based
    on US federal and majority-state law only.
    State the specific time period in years.'
-> re-run all 10 variants with this system prompt
-> compare agreement rate: with vs without prompt"

beats.narration_text: A well-anchored system prompt reduces variance. The agreement rate rises. But it does not go to one hundred percent — because the model is a probability distribution, not a lookup table. The lesson: prompt sensitivity is not a prompt engineering problem — it is a deployment risk measurement. For any high-stakes application, measure the agreement rate before shipping and set a minimum threshold it must clear.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: agreement rate before and after system prompt; 'LLM = probability distribution, not lookup table'

beats.narration_text: Your move: for any LLM application where consistency matters, build a semantic variant set for your top ten most important queries. Run this agreement matrix. Set a minimum agreement rate threshold — ninety percent for advisory use, ninety-five for consequential decisions. If you cannot meet it, add a human review step or constrain the output format to a structured field.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) build semantic variant set for key queries, 2) measure agreement rate, 3) set threshold per risk level

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: for any LLM application where consistency matters, build a semantic variant set for your top ten most important queries. Run this agreement matrix. Set a minimum agreement rate threshold — ninety percent for advisory use, ninety-five for consequential decisions. If you cannot meet it, add a human review step or constrain the output format to a structured field.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [LLM Prompt Sensitivity Tester: 10 Phrasings, How Many Answers?] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
